title: Cricket Bat Basics
answer: A cricket bat is usually made from willow. The key selection factors are pickup, weight balance, handle feel, and whether the blade shape suits front-foot or back-foot play. For most players, a bat that feels easy to control matters more than the heaviest possible bat.
examples: Tell me about cricket bat selection

title: Protective Equipment
aliases: what equipment is used in cricket
answer: Basic batting protection includes a helmet, batting pads, gloves, thigh guard, inner gloves, and an abdominal guard. Wicketkeepers use specialized gloves and pads, and close fielders may use helmets or shin guards depending on the situation.
examples: What equipment is used in cricket?

title: Batting Drills
answer: Good batting practice usually includes shadow batting, throwdowns, front-foot and back-foot movement drills, and controlled net sessions with a clear scoring zone target. Short, high-focus sessions work better than long unstructured hitting.

title: Bowling Drills
answer: Useful bowling drills include run-up rhythm checks, seam-position repetition, yorker target practice, slower-ball variations, and six-ball over plans. The best bowling practice is repeatable and target-based rather than just bowling fast.

title: Fielding Drills
answer: Effective fielding sessions combine high catches, slip-catching reaction work, one-hand pick-up and release drills, and direct-hit practice. Quality matters more than volume because fielding is about speed, clean technique, and repeatable execution.
examples: How do I practice fielding in cricket?

title: Practice Routine
answer: A solid cricket practice routine usually has four parts: warm-up and mobility, one skill block such as batting or bowling, one fielding block, and a short match-scenario block. Finishing with recovery and notes helps keep improvement measurable.
examples: Give me a cricket practice routine
